title: The Definition and Essence of Juice
body:
Definition
Juice means the game returning the maximum possible output in response to the player's minimal input.

"Juicy things are things that wobble, squirt, bounce around or make cute noises. Sort of a catch-all phrase for things that make the game feel good to interact with." — Martin Jonasson

The term originates from the classic Gamasutra article "How to prototype a game in under 7 days": "A juicy game feels alive and responds to everything you do — tons of cascading action and response for minimal user input."

Juice is primarily visual/auditory, but it doesn't have to be. Citing Emily Short's blog, Martin Jonasson explained that Juice exists even in interactive fiction (text-based games) — the witty, unexpected text response when the player attempts something unexpected. The essence of Juice is not confined to a particular medium.

Core principle
"Maximum output for minimum input." For a single button press, the game provides multi-layered reactions: visual (size, color, movement), auditory (sound effects), tactile (vibration). The player feels their actions genuinely affect the game world.

"If a game's juicy, it's way more fun to interact with it and it feels more professional. It doesn't matter if the graphics are crappy, but if it lacks that interactivity, it feels off." — Petri Purho
Juice is independent of graphics quality: crude graphics with Juice feel professional; gorgeous graphics without Juice feel like "something is missing."

Essential characteristics
1. It does not change the game's rules (mechanics) — Juice operates purely on the feedback layer. While adding Juice to a Breakout game, Martin Jonasson never once changed the rules.
2. The cumulative effect is large — any single effect is trivial, but overlapped effects change perceived quality dramatically; toggling all effects off/on at the end of the talk made it hard to believe it was the same game.
3. Its absence is immediately noticed — without Juice players feel it is "bland," "cheap," or "unresponsive." Jan Willem Nijman: "Without Juice, people say 'this game sucks'; with it, they just enjoy it without knowing why it's fun."
4. It is the most reliable means of improvement in game design — Petri Purho: "Game design is hard, and it's impossible to state a generality that fits every situation, but adding Juice is 100% guaranteed to make a game better."

Juice present vs. absent
| Situation | Without Juice | With Juice |
|---|---|---|
| Button click | The button changes to a pressed state | The button squashes and springs back, a click sound plays, tiny particles burst out, and the screen reacts ever so slightly |
| Killing an enemy | The enemy disappears | The enemy explodes, fragments scatter, the screen shakes, an impact sound plays, and the score pops up |
| Acquiring an item | The item is added to the inventory | The item glows and is sucked toward the player, an acquisition sound plays, and the inventory icon sparkles |

title: The Structure of Game Feel
body:
Definition
Game Feel means the tactile satisfaction the player feels when controlling the game. Also expressed as "the feel of the controls" (sonmat, "hand-taste" in Korean), it encompasses the responsiveness and sense of weight felt when controlling a game character or object via controller or keyboard/mouse.

Constituent elements
| Element | Definition | Good Example | Bad Example |
|---|---|---|---|
| Responsiveness | The latency between input and on-screen reaction | The character jumps the instant the button is pressed | Jump starts 0.3 seconds later |
| Weight | The degree to which an object feels as if it has physical mass | A greatsword is swung with heaviness | A greatsword is swung as lightly as a stick |
| Impact | The intensity of the shock delivered on collision/hit | A hit enemy is knocked back and the screen shakes | The enemy doesn't budge even when hit |
| Consistency | An always-predictable reaction to the same input | Jump height is always identical | Same button, but different result each time |

The relationship between Juice and Game Feel
- Game Feel is the result (the goal) the player feels.
- Juice is the means (the method) that produces that feeling.
If Game Feel is the final judgment of "this game feels good to control," then Juice is the collection of individual techniques — Screen Shake, particles, easing, etc. — used to elicit that judgment.

title: Feedback Loop Theory
body:
A core model that Jan Willem Nijman (Vlambeer) diagrammed directly in his talk.

Vlambeer's game loop model
Game --(visual/audio)--> Player's mind --(cognition/judgment)--> Player's action --(input/button)--> Game; and Player's action --(thought/meaning)--> Gameplay.
1. Game -> Player: game state conveyed through sight and sound.
2. Player -> Game: pressing a button conveys input to the game.
3. Game -> Player (change): the game changes and that change is conveyed back. Vlambeer focuses precisely on strengthening this stage.
4. Inside the player (thought/meaning): thought occurs and meaning is assigned to the game. Jan Willem: "This is where the true value of a game is created. People press a button, the game changes, and through that their life changes and they can learn something."

Core insight
"I press the button, the bullet flies out, the bullet hits something — making this process good is what we do." — Jan Willem Nijman
Vlambeer specialized in "the part where the game returns change to the player." This is Game Feel, and strengthening the output (feedback) stage of this loop is the essence of Juice.

The importance of meaning
After applying all 30 techniques, Jan Willem finally added "Meaning" — he made the enemy able to kill the player and added a Game Over screen: "When you make a game, make the thing you want to happen in it actually happen. Stuff like 'if you get hit by an enemy, press restart' is garbage. Program that Game Over. Give the game meaning." Juice is not mere decoration but a means of strengthening the game's communication of meaning.

Juice is not the source of fun — it is an amplifier. Fun originates in the player's sense of achievement from solving a problem with their own brain and controls. If the system is so simple it's fully understood the moment it opens, even the flashiest effects get boring fast. Conversely, when you pose a worthwhile problem and react to input with Screen Shake, particles, and crunchy sound, juice amplifies that achievement explosively. The key is the combination of "a problem worth solving + intense feedback" (for difficulty itself, see cognitive load in UIUX_Visual_Theory_Trends_EN).

The multi-layered structure of feedback — for a single action, provide feedback across multiple channels simultaneously:
| Channel | Role | Example (on hitting an enemy) |
|---|---|---|
| Visual — direct | Immediate change of the target | Hit flash on the enemy (white flash), knockback |
| Visual — environmental | Reaction of the surrounding world | Screen Shake, particle burst |
| Visual — UI | Information display | Damage number popup, health bar decrease |
| Auditory | Sound feedback | Impact sound, enemy death sound |
| Tactile | Physical feedback | Haptic vibration (mobile/console) |
| Temporal | Time manipulation | Hit pause (0.02 sec freeze) |

Interrupt/restart the current tween on hit: at the moment the target is struck, kill the in-progress tween (movement, idle, FX) and immediately override it with the hit reaction. If you restart the white flash (1–2 frames) + knockback as a fresh tween, the hit isn't buried under the prior motion and is clearly perceived. Insisting on smooth interpolation only mushes the impact.

Vlambeer's development timeline philosophy
"We build the basics of a game in a few days, then polish for a year." — Jan Willem Nijman. The first prototype and the final product are mechanically nearly identical; that year is spent strengthening the feedback loop.

title: Control Feel
body:
While Juice covers the game's output (feedback/presentation), Control Feel covers the opposite side — the sensation of the player's input being converted into the character. This is closer to the essence of Game Feel as Steve Swink described it: no matter how flashy the presentation, it falls flat if the control itself feels sluggish.

4-1. The cognitive correction loop & latency budget
The human correction loop — perceive the screen -> interpret in the brain -> send feedback to the finger muscles — takes about 240ms. A game must operate within this loop to feel "like my own body."
| Criterion | Value | Meaning |
|---|---|---|
| Cognitive correction loop | ~240ms | One cycle of perceive -> interpret -> muscle response |
| Minimum smoothness | >=10 FPS | Lower bound for perceiving motion as "continuous" |
| Continuity limit | input -> output within 100ms | Response limit for feeling control continuity (same as UIUX_Visual_Theory_Trends_EN §1-1 ECN 100ms) |
When this loop is kept tight, the player perceives the character as an extension of their own body. If latency exceeds the budget, the sense of "I am controlling this" breaks.

4-2. Animation lock-out vs cancel
Whether to block input until the motion ends (lock-out) or allow interrupting it anytime (cancel) is a responsiveness <-> weight tradeoff.
| Structure | Trait | Effect |
|---|---|---|
| Fixed (Lock-out) | No control until the motion ends | Adds heft and the weight of commitment, lowers responsiveness (Souls-like attacks) |
| Cancel system | Can cancel mid-action into another input (dodge, etc.) | Raises responsiveness, invites reflexes, raises the skill ceiling (action/fighting games) |
Intentional constraint: lock-out isn't inherently bad. Combined with a systemic context that demands reading enemy patterns and precise timing, that "no-control window" instead creates intended tension and depth. The width and timing of the cancel window is what determines that game's feel (input buffering -> see Hit Stop).

4-3. The ADSR input framework
Maps audio synthesis's ADSR onto control feel to design a character's sense of motion.
| Element | Meaning | Design point |
|---|---|---|
| Attack (rise) | Time to reach max output (max speed, etc.) after input | Instant = stiff/precise / a subtle acceleration curve = smooth, lively motion |
| Dimensionality | The number of states/dimensions the input device has | Digital button (on/off) vs analog stick (continuous) — different expressive range |
| Sensitivity | Output reaction strength relative to input magnitude | Tune precision vs dynamism via the response curve (linear/exponential) |
| Versatility | The creative-control Possibility Space built from combining a single input | Direction changes, combo links, aerial-move combinations, etc. — wider = more room for mastery |
Even just adding subtle acceleration/deceleration to Attack makes the same movement speed feel completely different. Input feel is the primary game feel, decided before any presentation.
Applying acceleration & friction (in practice): snapping velocity straight to max on input, or to 0 on stop, feels mechanical. Interpolating gradually toward a target speed (e.g. Godot move_toward, or `v += (target - v) * k` per frame) grants mass and inertia without changing the max-speed value. Design the friction (deceleration) curve symmetrically with acceleration (Attack).

4-4. Input forgiveness
Invisible systems that quietly correct the player's input error — removing the unfairness of "I definitely pressed it but it didn't work" and raising control comfort. A platformer essential.
- Coyote Time: a timer that keeps the jump input valid for a very short window (typically ~0.1 sec) even just after the character has walked off the edge of a platform into the air. Removes the frustration of falling when you barely step off a ledge.
- Jump Buffering: temporarily stores a jump input pressed just before landing (while still airborne) in a buffer, and auto-fires it the instant the character lands. Keeps a slightly-too-early input from being dropped.
- Both timers are best when "invisible" — the player feels "the controls are precise" without ever noticing the correction. (Combat input buffering -> Hit Stop.)

Beyond input and presentation, the fusion of systems and game feel (push-forward combat, reward-driven play inducement, dynamic rhythm quantization, etc.) is covered in the separate doc Systemic_Game_Feel_EN.

title: Tweening / Easing
body:
Definition
Tweening is short for "in-betweening," a technique that automatically computes intermediate values between two states to create a smooth transition. Easing is the function that makes this transition accelerate or decelerate rather than move at a constant speed.

Why it matters
Objects in the real world never move at a constant speed. Throw a ball and it's fast at first then gradually slows; a falling object gradually speeds up. Linear movement gives a "mechanical," "cheap" feel. Martin Jonasson also emphasized the importance of delay: instead of all blocks appearing simultaneously, having them appear with a slight time offset (random delay) makes for far more sophisticated presentation.

The simplest EaseOut implementation (the one-line Lerp formula)
The minimal implementation Martin Jonasson introduced as "just remember this" — run every frame to move x 10% toward target:
x += (target - x) * 0.1f;
This single line gives a natural deceleration effect: fast at first, slowing as it nears the target. Useful when you can't use a tweening engine or when prototyping quickly.
"You can't always use a tweening engine. There are times when it's not appropriate, or you're lazy, or whatever, but you can always use this baby right here." — Martin Jonasson

Easing function reference
| Easing Type | Movement Characteristic | Primary Use Case | Frequency of Use |
|---|---|---|---|
| Linear | Constant speed from start to finish | Almost never used | 1/5 |
| EaseOut | Fast start -> gradually slows | Landing, arrival, UI entrance | 5/5 |
| EaseIn | Slow start -> gradually speeds up | Acceleration, energy charging, UI exit | 3/5 |
| EaseInOut | Slow->fast->slow | Sophisticated transitions, menus | 4/5 |
| Back | Slightly overshoots the target then returns | Popup entrance, button click | 4/5 |
| Bounce | Bounces after arrival | Item acquisition, a playful feel | 3/5 |
| Elastic | Wobbles after arrival | Warning messages, emphasis | 2/5 |

Parameters
Button hover: original size 100% -> 110% on hover; Easing: EaseOut; Duration: 0.15 sec.
Popup entrance: Scale 0% -> 100% (temporarily ~105% due to Overshoot); Easing: Back (Overshoot 0.3); Duration: 0.3 sec.
Sequential entrance delay: delay between each element 0.03–0.05 sec; for 20 elements total, full entrance time 0.6–1.0 sec.

title: Squash & Stretch
body:
Definition
One of Disney's 12 principles of animation. A technique that depicts an object squashing or stretching as it moves or collides.

Why it matters
This technique imbues an object with a sense of material and weight. A hard object deforms barely at all; a soft object deforms a lot. Martin Jonasson applied this to the paddle and ball, demonstrating how the Breakout game's objects became lively, like jelly.

Core principle
Volume conservation: when squashed vertically (Squash), it stretches horizontally; when stretched vertically (Stretch), it narrows horizontally.

Parameters
| Style | X Deformation | Y Deformation | Suitable Games |
|---|---|---|---|
| Realistic | 90–110% | 110–90% | Simulation, sports |
| Cartoon | 70–130% | 130–70% | Casual, platformer |
| Exaggerated | 50–150% | 150–50% | Comic, party games |

Ball collision sequence:
1. Before collision: circular (100%, 100%)
2. Moment of collision: flat (130%, 70%) — Duration: 0.02 sec
3. On bouncing off: elongated (80%, 120%) — Duration: 0.05 sec
4. Settle: return to circular (100%, 100%) — Easing: EaseOut, Duration: 0.1 sec

Button click sequence:
1. Before press: default size (100%, 100%)
2. While pressing: squashed (105%, 95%) — Duration: 0.05 sec
3. Moment of release: stretched (95%, 105%) — Duration: 0.05 sec
4. Return: (100%, 100%) — Easing: EaseOut, Duration: 0.1 sec

Character locomotion (jump/land) sequence — applying it to a character is where the bounciest sense of motion comes alive. Leave the data (jump height/speed) unchanged and create the "springiness" through staging alone:
1. Just before jump (crouch): squashed down (110%, 90%) — Duration: 0.05 sec
2. Rising: stretched up (90%, 110%) — the faster the speed, the more elongated
3. Apex: return to circular (100%, 100%)
4. Moment of landing: pressed down hard (120%, 80%) — Duration: 0.05 sec
5. Return: (100%, 100%) — Easing: EaseOut (slight Back) or an Elastic tween, Duration: 0.1–0.15 sec

Cautions
Separate the hitbox (essential): squash & stretch scales only the sprite, leaving the hitbox (collision) at its original shape — it improves the feel/motion without affecting gameplay logic.
The same principle applies on an enemy hit: momentarily flatten it sideways (130%, 70%) then return it, and it reacts "like a living organism."

Dash staging recipe — a core indie technique. Without changing the movement-speed value at all, staging alone creates the illusion that it's "much faster and snappier":
- Stretch: at the dash moment, stretch long in the travel direction (e.g. 150%, 70%), with an EaseOut return on end.
- Afterimage: 3–5 silhouette afterimages behind the character at 0.1–0.2 sec intervals (fading opacity), or wind/speed-line particles (Trail particles).
- Camera: speed-linked FOV/zoom to amplify the perceived speed.
- Adding a short hit-stop (0.02–0.03 sec) or a brief freeze on the start frame gives a "pop-out" snap.

title: Particle Systems
body:
Definition
A technique that creates visual effects by generating large numbers of small particles on screen.

Why it matters
"You can never have too many particles." — Martin Jonasson
Particles add visual weight to a game's "action." An enemy simply disappearing versus exploding with fragments flying gives a completely different sense of satisfaction.

Programmer trap warning
"The particle engine is a programmer's trap. You get sucked into building the best particle engine and end up never actually using it in the game. Take something already made and use it." — Martin Jonasson
Use a proven particle system (Unity's built-in Particle System, VFX Graph, etc.) and do not waste time building a custom engine.

Design guide by type
1) Collision/Hit particles
- Fragments, sparks, dust
- Particles should spread in the opposite direction of the collision
- Light hit: 5–10 / Strong hit: 20–50
- Lifetime: 0.3–0.8 sec

2) Trail particles
- Afterimages, light streaks, smoke tails
- Trail length increases proportionally to movement speed
- Opacity: decreases over time (1.0 -> 0.0)
- Color: choose a color that matches the object

3) Environmental particles
- Dust, light motes, falling leaves, snow, rain
- Maintain a moderate density without obstructing vision
- Play continuously, independent of player interaction
- Serve to add a sense of depth to the background

4) Reward/Acquisition particles
- Sparkles, stars, light bursts
- Increase spectacle in proportion to the reward's rarity:
  - Common: small sparkle (3–5)
  - Rare: light burst (10–20) + weak Screen Shake
  - Legendary: golden burst (30–50) + strong Screen Shake + special sound

5) Destruction particles
- The key point Martin Jonasson emphasized: instead of a block simply disappearing, presentation where it falls or shatters according to physical laws
- Apply gravity and rotation to fragments
- Fragment size is 10–30% of the original
- If fragments remain on the floor, they create synergy with the Permanence effect

title: Screen Shake
body:
Definition
A technique that shakes the entire screen on a powerful collision, explosion, or hit to indirectly convey physical shock to the player.

Why it matters
Jan Willem Nijman called it "the symbol of impact": "When someone shows a game and asks 'how do I improve it?', the answer is usually 'add Screen Shake.' Nobody does it, it's super easy, and the game gets better."
Martin Jonasson, describing the moment of turning on Screen Shake in his Breakout demo: "The ball went from a stupid tennis ball to a motherfucking comet."
The human brain interprets visual shaking as "physical shock." Even with no other effects, just Screen Shake brings the impact to life; conversely, no matter how gorgeous the particles, without Screen Shake it feels flat.
Jan Willem's conviction: "Probably every game would benefit from Screen Shake. Think 'my puzzle game doesn't need Screen Shake'? You're wrong."

Parameters
| Parameter | Description | Weak | Medium | Strong |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Intensity | Maximum displacement of the shake | 2–5px | 5–15px | 15–30px |
| Duration | How long the shake lasts | 0.05–0.1 sec | 0.1–0.3 sec | 0.3–0.5 sec |
| Decay | Intensity reduction over time | Exponential decay recommended | Exponential decay | Exponential decay |
| Frequency | Shakes per second | 30–60Hz | 30–60Hz | 30–60Hz |

Cautions
1. Random direction: shake in a 360-degree random direction, not just simple left-right, for a natural feel.
2. Decay is essential: strong at first, gradually weaker. Without decay it feels mechanical.
3. Accumulate multiple shakes: when several shakes occur simultaneously, sum the intensity but set an upper limit (max 40px recommended).
4. Always provide an option: provide intensity adjustment or an off option for players who report dizziness. Vlambeer actually had to add a Screen Shake off option in Nuclear Throne. Jan Willem's warning: "Screen Shake is addictive. Once you get used to it, you keep cranking it up, and you don't feel it but other people vomit. Then you do playtesting and... you ignore everyone and just keep it (laughs). Seriously, don't put in anything that physically makes people sick."
5. On mobile, reduce sensitivity to 70–80%. The screen is small, so the same px feels more intense.

title: Hit Stop / Freeze Frame
body:
Definition
A technique that freezes the game very briefly (about 0.02–0.05 sec) at the moment of impact to give time to perceive the shock.

Why it matters
The human brain has difficulty perceiving moments that pass quickly. Hit pause signals to the brain that "this moment is important." It is a core element of impact in fighting games and action games.
"At the moment of impact, you freeze the game for about 20 milliseconds. The brain doesn't perceive it, but uses that time to better process what's happening. At every moment you want to give impact, the brain goes 'oh, I remember that.' Because it took longer. But you don't feel it." — Jan Willem Nijman
He also shared a framerate trick: dropping the framerate to 10fps at the moment of dying to an enemy enables dramatic presentation without a separate slow-motion system.

Parameters
| Situation | Freeze Time | Notes |
|---|---|---|
| Normal hit | 0.02–0.03 sec (1–2 frames @60fps) | Subtle but felt |
| Heavy hit/skill | 0.05–0.08 sec (3–5 frames) | Clear impact |
| Critical/finisher | 0.1–0.2 sec (6–12 frames) | Dramatic presentation |

It applies beyond combat hits — to strong traversal actions like jumps and dashes, too. Dropping the time scale to near 0 for a few frames at that moment, then restoring it, sharpens the separation of inputs and the sense of impact through a split-second pause the player never consciously notices.

Cautions
- Input buffering: store player input during the hit pause and execute it immediately after the pause is released.
- UI keeps running: UI animation must not stop even during the hit pause.
- When adjusting Time.timeScale, separate the UI using unscaledDeltaTime (Unity-specific).

title: Knockback
body:
Definition
An effect that visualizes the transfer of physical force, pushing the struck target away in the direction of the hit.

Why it matters
Jan Willem Nijman presented it as one of his 30 tips. Knockback adds dynamism to combat and gives the feeling that "my attack actually carries physical force."

Design philosophy: "Create a reason not to press the button"
"If you put a fire button in your game, also create a reason not to press it. Otherwise, you're better off removing the fire button and making it auto-fire." — Jan Willem Nijman
Player Knockback is the realization of this principle:
- Firing the gun pushes the player backward -> forward speed slows
- Therefore the player must strategically judge "should I fire now or not"
- Firing while airborne extends hang time via recoil, and other new mechanics emerge
- All of this is Juice and gameplay at the same time

Parameters
| Situation | Pushback Distance | Pushback Time |
|---|---|---|
| Normal hit | 0.3–0.5 units | 0.1 sec |
| Heavy hit | 1.0–2.0 units | 0.15 sec |
| Explosion | 3.0–5.0 units | 0.2–0.3 sec |

- Easing: EaseOut (pushed away quickly, then gradually stops)
- Player Recoil: making the player too be pushed back slightly when firing lets the gun's power be felt (Jan Willem Nijman).

title: Camera Techniques (Lerp, Look-Ahead, Kick, Zoom, Speed-Reactive FOV)
body:
7-1. Camera Interpolation (Lerp): The camera follows the player smoothly, lagging slightly behind rather than rigidly chasing.
- Lerp value: 0.1-0.3 (the lower, the smoother and looser)
- Too loose loses the target, so avoid values below 0.05.

7-2. Look Ahead Camera: Offset the camera to show more of what's ahead in the aiming direction or movement direction.
Jan Willem Nijman: "This game isn't about showing the player. This game is about shooting enemies, and the enemies are ahead. So show more of what's ahead."
Advanced application in Luftrausers: the camera doesn't just follow the player; it shifts slightly toward the aiming direction, moves down when approaching the water to signal "there's water here", and computes the centroid of all bullets so the camera shifts slightly, letting the player dodge even off-screen bullets.
- Offset distance: movement speed x 0.5-1.0 units
- Transition Easing: EaseOut, Duration: 0.3 sec

7-3. Camera Kick: A core technique of Jan Willem Nijman. Momentarily push the camera in the direction opposite to firing to visualize recoil.
- Kick distance: 3-10px
- Return: EaseOut, Duration: 0.1 sec

7-4. Zoom In/Out:
- Zoom in on a finishing move: Scale 1.0 -> 1.2, Duration: 0.2 sec
- Zoom out on an explosion: Scale 1.0 -> 0.9, Duration: 0.3 sec
- Return: EaseOut, Duration: 0.5 sec

7-5. Speed-Reactive FOV / Zoom: Where 7-4 is event-driven (one-shot moments like a finish or explosion), this camera reacts continuously to movement speed. The faster the player goes, the wider the view (or a slight zoom-out) to amplify the sense of speed — especially effective on boosts, dashes, and falls.
- Principle: normalize current speed to 0-1, then map it to FOV (3D) or orthographic camera size / zoom (2D).
- 3D FOV: base 60 deg -> 70-80 deg at top speed. Widen with EaseOut on acceleration; return more slowly (EaseInOut) on deceleration to avoid bobbing.
- 2D zoom: base 1.0 -> 0.9-0.95 (slight zoom-out) at high speed. Interpolate orthographic size proportionally to speed.
- Smoothing is mandatory: Lerp the FOV/zoom value itself (0.05-0.1) so it never snaps. Jumpy values cause motion sickness.
- Cap & option: cap the maximum swing, and — like Screen Shake — expose an intensity/off option (protects motion-sensitive players).

In racers, runners, and shmups, half of the feeling of "fast" comes from this speed-linked FOV. At the same actual speed, a wider view spikes the perceived speed.

title: Sound Design (Bass Boost, Pitch Randomization vs Build-Up, Pooling)
body:
Why it matters: Martin Jonasson demonstrated that adding only sound to the same animation makes the player's brain interpret it completely differently. Circles passing over each other are perceived as "colliding" with one another from a single sound alone.

Sound design principles:

1) Give every interaction a sound
- Button click, swipe, hit, getting hit, item acquisition, level-up, etc.
- Silence = absence of feedback = emptiness

2) Convey emotion through sound
- Positive actions: bright, high tone (melodic, pleasant feel)
- Negative actions: low, heavy tone (warning, failure)
- Reward: rising melody (anticipation -> achievement)

3) Reinforce the bass
A true story Jan Willem Nijman shared: "A senior designer at Raven Software told me this. One gun in an FPS game wasn't popular, so the investor said 'fix the gameplay.' He opened Audacity, applied a 12dB bass boost to the gun's sound effect, and put it back in the game. He handed it to the investor, who said 'oh, that's good now.' And that sound became one of the most popular guns in that game."
What this anecdote shows: simply boosting a sound's bass makes players feel "the gameplay improved." Players perceive a change in sound as a change in the quality of the game itself.
- Add sub-bass (60-100Hz) to hit and explosion sounds
- Jan Willem's demo: after comparing the same firing sound before and after bass reinforcement and asking "which one do you want to play?", the entire audience chose the bass-reinforced version

4) Sound pooling
- Limit simultaneous playback of the same sound (max 3-5)
- When the same sound repeats, randomly vary the pitch by +/-5-8% to avoid monotony

5) Pitch Build-Up (rising/escalation)
- In situations where a chain stacks up — combos, consecutive pickups, score tallies — step the pitch up progressively at each step to build a sense of escalation. (Peggle's score tally and Mario's consecutive coin sound are the canonical examples.)
- Do not confuse with the random pitch in (4): randomization is for preventing repetition fatigue (removing the monotony of the same sound); build-up is for creating a sense of rise (the mounting pleasure of stacking). Their purposes are opposite.
- Applies to: number count-up (UI_Feedback_Design_Guide_EN Number Change), reward tallies, combo counters.

title: Haptic Feedback (Pattern Table, Off Option, Battery)
body:
Definition: A technique that conveys physical sensation through the vibration of a touchscreen or controller.

Why it matters (2026 trend): It is the lowest-utilized yet highly effective feedback channel in mobile games. Using all three channels — visual + auditory + tactile — simultaneously dramatically increases the volume of information the brain receives, raising immersion. As the HD vibration of the DualSense controller shows, haptic technology is evolving from simple "buzzing" to detailed texture expression. On mobile too, iOS's Taptic Engine and Android's vibration API support sophisticated patterns.

Design guide:

| Situation | Haptic Pattern | Intensity | Duration |
|------|------|------|------|
| Button tap confirmation | Short single vibration | Light | 10ms |
| Successful hit | Strong single vibration | Medium-Heavy | 20-30ms |
| Critical hit | Strong vibration + brief pause + weak vibration | Heavy | 50ms |
| Skill charge complete | Gradually intensifying vibration | Light->Heavy | 200ms |
| Error/failure | Quick double vibration | Medium | 30ms x 2 |
| Item acquisition | Rising pattern | Light->Medium | 100ms |

Cautions:
- Excessive vibration drains the battery quickly. Apply it only to essential interactions.
- Always provide an off option.
- Use it as a supplementary means to visual/auditory feedback; do not convey information through haptics alone.

title: Permanence (Ragdoll, Ash-Dissolve, Shell Casings, Hotline Miami)
body:
Definition: A technique that keeps the results of in-game actions (corpses, fragments, shell casings, etc.) from disappearing, leaving them on the floor to show the traces of the combat the player has passed through.

Why it matters: One of Jan Willem Nijman's core tips. Permanence visually provides the player with the sense of achievement of "I accomplished this much."
Jan Willem Nijman: "Why leave the battlefield empty after combat ends? I killed 1, 2, 3, 4, 5, 6, 7 of them here. There was a battle here. Later, when you walk back through this nice level-design corridor, you can tell 'ah, I've already been here.'"
Cinematic analogy: "The reason a bar shootout in a movie is cool is that every stray bullet actually hits a bottle."
Application in Nuclear Throne: background objects like cacti don't affect gameplay, but they're destroyed when hit by stray bullets. Such environmental destruction leaves traces of combat and gives the world life.

Application examples:
- Enemy corpses: maintained without fading for 2-3 seconds after death, then gradually become transparent (or kept permanently)
- Ragdoll (physics corpse): when an enemy dies, don't just let it vanish; let it fly off and collapse according to physical laws in the direction of the hit. Passing the knockback vector straight through as the death impulse carries the weight of the blow all the way into death. The fallen corpse remains as permanence (a trace).
- Ash-dissolve fallback: on performance-constrained setups where leaving a physical corpse is hard, substitute a flashy-particle ash dissolve that scatters into ash. No trace remains, but the "definitely dead" impact is preserved.
- Shell casings: Jan Willem: "Computers these days can leave casings forever. They don't need to disappear. You could cover the floor with casings, and later when you come back after beating a boss, count the bullets and go 'ah, I shot a lot here.'"
- Fragments: fragments of destroyed objects remain on the floor
- Environmental destruction: bullet marks on walls, floor cracks, destruction of background objects
- Hotline Miami reference: Jan Willem cited Hotline Miami as the best example of permanence. Clearing all enemies on one floor, going up to the next and killing more, then walking back down through a sea of corpses — using this in a meaningful way gives the player something to think about.

Performance considerations:
- Limit maximum quantity with object pooling (50-100)
- Fade out the oldest debris sequentially
- Apply LOD (Level of Detail): simplify distant debris

title: Personality (Kyle Gabler Trick, Eye Tracking, Idle Variations, Barks)
body:
Definition: A technique that breathes emotion and life into game objects.

Why it matters: Martin Jonasson introduced this as the "Kyle Gabler trick." Kyle Gabler, the developer of World of Goo, is the person who showed that simply adding eyes to a game object can create a dramatic emotional connection. Martin demonstrated that with the tiny effort of drawing eyes and a mouth on the Breakout game's paddle, players became emotionally connected to the character. Blinking can be implemented simply by briefly disabling the sprite.
"Let's make the eyes a little bigger. So much better with bigger eyes." — Martin Jonasson
A terrified expression when the ball moves away, a smiling expression when it catches the ball — the personality payoff relative to this programming/art investment is enormous.

Methods of application:

1) Eye Tracking
- Make the object's eyes follow the object of interest (ball, enemy, cursor)
- Gaze-movement Lerp: 0.1-0.2

2) Situational Expressions
- In danger: anxious expression
- On success: joyful expression
- While idle: sleepy/bored expression (Idle variation)

3) Idle Animation Variations
- The size subtly changes as if breathing even while still
- Scale: 100% <-> 101-102%, Duration: 2-3 sec, Easing: EaseInOut
- Play a special Idle animation after a period with no input

4) Contextual Barks
- Pop a short, distinctive line in a speech bubble / dialogue box that fits the moment to give the sense of "a living thing." (e.g. "Ow!" when hit, "Yes!" on a reward, mutterings while idle.)
- Combined with expressions and eye tracking, the effect multiplies — text alone grants a lot of personality at little cost.
- Avoid repetition: pick barks at random from a pool for the same situation and put a short cooldown so they don't spam (same principle as sound pooling -> T8).
- Entrance: a small pop (Scale 0->100%, Back), hold 2-3 sec, then fade out. Keep low-importance barks from covering gameplay.

title: Dynamic Lighting (Muzzle Halo, Screen Flash, Photosensitivity Warning)
body:
Definition: A technique that reacts dynamic light sources to actions to amplify the power of a weapon/magic/explosion with light. If particles convey "quantity," lighting conveys "energy."

Why it matters: Even the same muzzle flash feels completely different when it's a plain sprite versus when it's a light source that actually illuminates the surrounding walls/character/floor. Light instantly tells the brain "powerful energy was just released." In Deepnight's demo too, the moment lighting is turned on, the same-mechanic gun feels far more powerful.

Techniques:

1) Muzzle halo / momentary light
- At the moment of firing/hitting, flicker a bright halo ring around the character for 0.05-0.1 sec.
- Briefly toggle a point light on and off to actually illuminate the surroundings. Intensity decays with EaseOut.

2) Full-screen momentary flash
- On a powerful action (big explosion, ultimate, finisher), flash the whole screen bright for just a moment (1-2 frames, 0.03-0.05 sec).
- White or the action color (explosion = orange, magic = element color). Return immediately with EaseOut.
- Do not overuse: used too often it fatigues the eyes and buries information. Reserve it for decisive moments.

3) Emissive objects <-> lighting system real-time interaction
- Have fire/lava/magic/glowing projectiles contribute as real light sources to the game lighting to raise the world's realism.
- When a projectile passes by, the walls/floor it passes are briefly tinted.

Parameters / Cautions:

| Situation | Intensity/Duration | Note |
|------|------|------|
| Normal-fire muzzle halo | Small radius, 0.05 sec | Brief point light |
| Strong hit/skill | Medium radius + color tint, 0.1 sec | Environment reflection |
| Big explosion/finisher | Full-screen flash 0.03-0.05 sec | Decisive moments only |

- Eye fatigue & accessibility: flashes and strong light carry a risk of photosensitive seizures. Cap the frequency/intensity and always provide a flash-reduce/off option (same principle as the Screen Shake option).
- Performance: real-time dynamic lights are expensive. On mobile/low-end, limit the light count or substitute fake lighting (sprite glow).

title: Practical Application Workflow (4 Phases)
body:
Phase 1: Build Foundational Infrastructure (Priority: Highest)
First, build the systems that form the base of all UI feedback.

| System | Description | Recommended Tool (Unity) |
|------|------|------|
| Tweening system | The base of all animation | DOTween (free/paid) |
| Audio manager | SFX playback, pooling, volume control | Self-built or FMOD |
| Base button component | 5-state implementation, reusable | Custom UI component |

Phase 2: Core Juice Systems (Priority: High)
The core that determines a game's impact and responsiveness.

| System | Description | Key Requirements |
|------|------|------|
| Particle system | Per-situation presets, pooling | Object pooling essential |
| Screen Shake system | Supports intensity/duration/decay | Manage accumulation of multiple shakes |
| Hit pause system | Time-scale adjustment | Input buffering, UI separation |
| Sound feedback system | Per-situation SFX mapping | Pitch randomization, pooling |

Phase 3: Polishing (Priority: Medium)
The detail elements that raise the game's level of finish.

| System | Description |
|------|------|
| Character personality | Eye tracking, situational expressions, Idle variations |
| Environmental particles | Background dust, light motes, weather |
| Advanced camera techniques | Look ahead camera, camera kick, cinematics |
| Haptic feedback | Design mobile vibration patterns |
| Permanence system | Corpse/fragment persistence, pooling management |

Phase 4: Apply 2026 Trends (Priority: Optional)
Apply the latest trends to differentiate the game.

| Application Item | Difficulty | Effect |
|------|------|------|
| Glassmorphism HUD | Medium | Visual refinement + immersion |
| Adaptive UI | High | Personalized experience |
| Reduced-motion option | Low | Improved accessibility |
| Multimodal feedback matrix | Medium | Maximized perceived quality |
| Diegetic UI | High | Maximized immersion |

title: Golden Rule: Scale Juice to the Screen's Purpose
body:
A single principle that threads through all the anti-patterns. It extends AP2's (excessive feedback) concept of "hierarchy" to the screen level.

Juice intensity should be proportional to the screen's purpose — the more important, the bigger.

- Reward/celebration UI (level-up, reward tally, victory screen): use big, exaggerated juice freely. Here, exaggeration is a virtue.
- Precision/input-heavy UI (forms, drag, text entry, competitive HUD): keep it calm and steady. Strong motion hurts readability and accuracy, so restrain it.
- Always gate strong motion behind a reduce-motion / intensity option, and match the weight of the feedback = the weight of the action.

The same effect can be right or wrong depending on which screen it's on. "What does this screen ask the player to do?" is the yardstick for juice intensity. (ECN-DMN balance -> UIUX_Visual_Theory_Trends_EN)

title: Honest Juice (Intensity = Value of the Actual Accomplishment)
body:
If the golden rule is "intensity = the screen's purpose," this principle is "intensity = the value of the actual accomplishment." The splendor of the presentation must honestly match the scale of what the player actually achieved, so that trust is built. (Source: Ethical Engagement Game Feel Framework v1.0)

- Salience honesty: A golden spark burst is matched only to high-difficulty boss clears and monumental feats; tier color is matched only to an item's actual rarity. When visual intensity exceeds real importance, it breeds misreading of information and distrust.
- Sound honesty: Give sound that matches the nature of the result, and mix the pre-reveal build-up before opening a box (-> T8) so it balances with the actual range of the reward's value. A gorgeous presentation must not glorify a poor result.
- Honest portrayal of disappointing results: When a goal is missed, do not dress it up in an exaggerated upward presentation, but transparently show the accumulated milestone for the challenge itself (a pity gauge, etc.).
- Attribution of achievement: Place the center of catharsis on the player's proactive decisions and results. Give feedback that supports wise choices such as resupplying or retreating as well.

Final quality question: Does the Juice on this screen honestly amplify the joy of the pure achievement the player earned?

title: Anti-Patterns AP1-AP7
body:
AP1: Interaction Without Feedback
Problem: Pressing a button gives no reaction, or you can't tell whether you hit an enemy.
Symptoms: The player clicks multiple times wondering "did that click register?" Feedback like "there's no impact," "it feels empty."
Solution: Add at least a visual change + sound to every interaction element. Use the checklist.

AP2: Excessive Feedback
Problem: Screen Shake on everything, particles everywhere, endless sound effects.
Symptoms: Complaints of dizziness. Missing important information. Effects become dull, so nothing feels special.
Solution: Place a hierarchy on feedback intensity: normal action < important action < decisive moment. Use strong effects like Screen Shake only at truly important moments. Provide an intensity-adjustment option.

AP3: Inconsistent Feedback
Problem: A loud sound effect for a small action, mismatched timing between visual and sound.
Symptoms: A vague displeasure that "something feels off." Conveying a feeling different from the intent.
Solution: Match the "weight" of the action to the "weight" of the feedback. Synchronize visual and auditory timing at the frame level.

AP4: Abrupt Transitions
Problem: The screen switches instantly with no fade, or the mode changes without warning.
Symptoms: Startle, broken immersion, confusion.
Solution: Add a fade or animation to every screen transition. Pre-announce mode transitions (music change, visual hint). Buffer interval: combat ends -> victory presentation -> resume exploration.

AP5: Information Overload
Problem: So much UI on screen that important things can't be identified.
Symptoms: "I don't know what to look at." Rapid increase in fatigue.
Solution: Keep UI footprint at 30% or less. Build a visual hierarchy based on information priority. Hide unnecessary UI depending on the situation.

AP6: Mobile-Specific Anti-Patterns
Problem: Applying a PC-designed UI directly to mobile.
Symptoms: Buttons too small, frequent mis-taps. Excessive Screen Shake causes dizziness. UI obscures the screen, obstructing gameplay.
Solution: Touch area minimum 44x44pt. Reduce Screen Shake intensity to 70-80% on mobile. Manage screen footprint with collapsible/tab-based UI. UI re-layout logic when switching between portrait/landscape mode.

AP7: Loss of Objectivity About Your Own Game
A core anti-pattern emphasized in common by both Jan Willem Nijman and Brandon Chung.
Problem: The developer becomes too familiar with their own game, making objective judgment impossible.
Symptoms: Unconsciously avoiding bugs while playing. Thinking "this game is fun, right?" while others don't even know the controls. Cranking up Screen Shake more and more, but you don't feel it because you're used to it.
Solution: "You are the world's worst playtester. There are way more other people than you, so test on them." — Jan Willem Nijman
Regularly invite external playtesters and observe (have them play directly while you watch from beside them). Make the game you made "for yourself," but do testing "with other people." Especially for Game Feel-related values (Screen Shake intensity, hit pause length, etc.), external feedback is essential.

title: Jan Willem Nijman's Action Game Balancing Principles
body:
This crosses into game design beyond the realm of Juice, but these are principles you must know to maximize Juice's effect.

| Principle | Description | Reason |
|------|------|------|
| Lower enemy health | 6 low-health enemies over 1 high-health enemy | For the same total HP, killing multiple is more satisfying |
| Increase fire rate | Let the gun be fired more often | "If it's a shooting game, let them shoot a lot. That's the only way to communicate with the world" |
| Add more enemies | Create urgency through sheer volume | Particle and permanence effects are amplified doubly |
| Make bullets bigger | Real bullets are small, but in games make them at least the size of a character's torso | "Making bullets small in the game because they're small in reality is a beginner's mistake" |
| Add muzzle flash | Add a circle to the first frame of the bullet sprite | Maximum firing feel at minimum cost. A classic FPS technique |
| Increase bullet speed | Fast bullets = a powerful feel | Slow bullets feel sluggish and weak |
| Lower accuracy slightly | Let bullets spread a little | Different visual presentation each time, avoiding monotony |
| Increase spread on sustained fire (bloom) | The longer you hold fire, the wider the shot group spreads (military-FPS style) | Gives a "rough and powerful" weapon impression + discourages mindless full-auto, encouraging burst play |
| Add random explosions | 33% chance of explosion on enemy death | Visual pleasure even without a rational reason |
| Make explosions big | Big enough to occupy a substantial part of the screen | Jonathan Soderstrom (Cactus/Hotline Miami) advice: "Want to make a good arcade game? Make the explosions big" |

title: Essential Application Checklist (All Games)
body:
Buttons and interactions:
- [ ] Does every button have a Press/Release animation?
- [ ] Does every button have a click sound?
- [ ] Are disabled buttons clearly distinguished?
- [ ] PC: Is there a Hover state?
- [ ] Mobile: Is the touch area 44x44pt or larger?

Screen transitions:
- [ ] Does every screen transition have a Tween or Fade?
- [ ] Are there popup entrance/exit animations?
- [ ] Is there visual feedback indicating loading?

Number and state changes:
- [ ] Do number changes such as health and score have a counting animation?
- [ ] Do important state changes have a sound?
- [ ] Are buff/debuff applications displayed visually?

Reward/achievement:
- [ ] Is there a presentation on item acquisition?
- [ ] Is there special presentation on level-up/achievement unlock?
- [ ] Is there differentiated presentation by rarity?

Accessibility (2026 essential):
- [ ] Is there a Screen Shake intensity adjustment/off option?
- [ ] Is there a color-blindness mode?
- [ ] Is text size adjustable?
- [ ] Does every sound effect have an alternative visual feedback?

Juice honesty (-> Honest Juice):
- [ ] Does the achievement-celebration presentation correctly match the scale of the accomplishment actually achieved?
- [ ] Is a disappointing result expressed honestly without glorifying it with an exaggerated upward presentation?
- [ ] Is the splendor of the presentation honestly proportional to the actual value of the reward/item provided?
- [ ] Does the sound synchronize with the meaning of the actual event that occurred on the current screen?

title: Additional Checklist for Action/Combat Games
body:
- [ ] Is knockback implemented?
- [ ] Is hit pause implemented?
- [ ] Is there permanence (corpse, shell-casing persistence)?
- [ ] Are there enough particles?
- [ ] Is there additional feedback for combos/consecutive hits?
- [ ] Is there camera recoil/shake?
- [ ] Is there visual feedback (flash, blink) when an enemy is hit?
- [ ] Is a multimodal feedback matrix defined?

title: Additional Checklist for Healing/Casual Games
body:
- [ ] Is the UI minimized?
- [ ] Are transitions smooth and relaxed?
- [ ] Does it use soft feedback without strong Screen Shake?
- [ ] Is the sound comfortable and non-irritating?
- [ ] Are there no elements that rush the player?

title: Additional Checklist for Mobile Games (2026)
body:
- [ ] Are touch areas sufficient? (44x44pt / 48x48dp)
- [ ] Is haptic feedback applied to core interactions?
- [ ] Is Screen Shake intensity adjusted for mobile? (70-80%)
- [ ] Are particles/effects optimized considering battery consumption?
- [ ] Does the UI scale appropriately across various screen sizes (including foldables)?
- [ ] When using Glassmorphism, is performance acceptable on low-spec devices?

title: Core Thesis: Ethical Engagement Game Feel
body:
The true value of Game Feel lies in the player intuitively and joyfully receiving the system's feedback. The most ideal system is a structure that supports the player's process of immersing in a challenge with refined feedback.

Excellent engagement design gives the player clear goals, appropriate tension, the joy of achievement, and a sense of bonding with companions. Juice/UI/UX visually and aurally supports this emotional journey, sublimating mere system numbers into a sense of achievement, reward, the vibrancy of a live service, and a sense of growth.

Healthy, sustainable Game Feel amplifies the player's active choices and accomplishments, helping every moment of participation in the game become a voluntary and worthwhile journey.

Key distinction:

| Question | Sustainable, Immersive Game Feel | Intuition-Reinforcing UX Structure |
|---|---|---|
| What does it amplify? | Provides mastery, achievement, clear options | A sense of purpose, growth, positive bonding |
| What about the player's right to choose? | Free to rest and return at any time | On return, existing value and context are safely preserved |
| What is the value of the reward? | Proportional to actual difficulty, contribution, and the meaning of the choice | Provides satisfaction commensurate with the player's effort and journey |
| What is the role of the UI? | Transparency of information, status, results, and multifaceted options | Lays out every choice path intuitively and symmetrically |
| What does Juice present? | Meaningful play results and growth branch points | The moment the user clears a challenge and achieves a goal |

title: The 7-Stage Cyclical Engagement Loop
body:
The engagement loop that makes players deeply assimilate into the game's world and develop lasting affection generally operates meticulously in the order below. The more organically each stage is connected, the more the player continues voluntary play built on a sense of bonding.

2-1. Value Investment and Progress Formation
Encourage the player to build their own unique progression through time, effort, emotion, and social relationships.
Observable signals:
- Indicators that visualize long-term goals — season passes, guild progress, point accumulation, growth milestones — are active.
- Long-term progress graphs and presentation are provided so the player can intuitively perceive their own growth journey.
- Rewards are not severed but flow naturally into the next challenge and staged destination.
Sustainable use:
- Accumulated progress helps the player quickly recover context and re-acclimate when they return after a break.
- Even if the user rests for personal reasons, the core value and progress they have built up are not damaged in the meantime.

2-2. Opportunity and Information Guidance
At the point when the player wraps up or switches a particular piece of content, make them clearly aware of the benefits currently obtainable and information about the next opportunity.
Observable signals:
- At exit or screen transition, clearly remind the player of the remaining time of an ongoing event or a benefit nearing its deadline.
- Information on obtainable rewards and goal-achievement status appears in a visually clear contrast.
- Guidance messages are accompanied by intuitive, accurate numerical information to aid the user's reasonable judgment.
Mode of expression:
- It serves as useful navigation that helps the player not miss important in-game timings or opportunities.
- Rather than imposing compulsion, it transparently discloses the efficiency of choices so the user can make the best decision.

2-3. Responding to Anticipation
Heighten anticipation just before the moment of obtaining a reward or an achievement, so that the process itself is perceived as a thrilling play experience regardless of the value of the result.
Observable signals:
- In presentation segments such as roulette, card flips, and box openings, gradual sound build-up and smooth camera movement are used.
- When approaching a target value, visual effects that add to the thrill (particles, color shifts by tier, etc.) occur.
- Even if the result falls short of expectations, an encouraging message and remedial measures for the next challenge are visualized.
Mode of expression:
- Beyond a simple confirmation of the result, it gives the player the exhilarating tension and entertainment value of the moment just before the challenge.

2-4. Placing Balanced Hurdles
Precisely design hurdles — difficulty, time, strategic elements, etc. — to stimulate the player's appropriate desire for challenge and to maximize the sense of achievement.
Observable signals:
- At specific milestone segments, builds appear that demand the player's mastery and strategic judgment.
- Diverse alternatives exist for overcoming hurdles through optimized paths (knowing strategies, community cooperation, etc.).
- Hurdles are designed so their cause clearly matches the user's play style and strategic choices.
Mode of expression:
- A hurdle is branded not as a mere restriction but as a "strategic challenge" and a "growth-acceleration segment."
- Options for users who want rapid progress are proposed in clearly distinguished forms such as "boosters" and "time-saving convenience packages."

2-5. Providing Multifaceted Choice Paths
Lay out the optimal alternatives for resolving the situation the user faces in a visually and cognitively balanced way, guaranteeing the right to make a proactive choice.
Observable signals:
- Various resolution buttons — solving via in-game currency, revising strategy, applying convenience options — are placed with balanced visibility.
- The results and costs (in time/currency) each option brings to the user are compared at a glance without distortion.
- A flexible UX layout is provided so the user can customize the most comfortable flow to suit their play tendency.
Mode of expression:
- It gives the player the satisfaction that they "designed their own play path" entirely on their own initiative.
- The UI does not force any particular decision and supports all alternatives with equal weight.

2-6. Justifying the Sense of Achievement Through Juice
After a successful challenge and the selection of an option, concentrate rich feedback presentation so the user sensorially confirms that their action was a worthwhile accomplishment.
Observable signals:
- The instant a goal is achieved, gorgeous visual effects, a brisk signature sound, a victory fanfare, and device haptic responses are implemented simultaneously.
- When a stage rises or a value is reached, customized presentation emphasizing the user's contribution appears.
- Feedback that wholeheartedly celebrates the success of the optimized path the user chose is presented.
Mode of expression:
- By placing the center of catharsis on the user's proactive decisions and accomplishments, it makes the player feel a deep sense of growth and pride.

2-7. Internalizing a Daily Routine
Optimize the flow of live content so gameplay settles naturally into the user's daily life as a lively routine of fun.
Observable signals:
- Light daily challenges, weekly raids, and seasonal milestones are distributed and provided in harmony.
- Notifications or benefits the user has not yet checked are briefed at an optimal cadence that does not make the user feel burdened.
- As the user launches the game, they are pleasantly reminded of new content to enjoy today and the rewards to look forward to.
Mode of expression:
- Regular participation is expressed as "the liveliness of a live service" and "the warm solidarity among guild members."
- The fatigue that comes with long-term play is converted, within a range that does not hinder immersion, into a pleasant "sense of achievement and passion."

title: Value Preservation and a Sustainable Sense of Progress
body:
By thoroughly respecting and preserving the value of the assets and time the user has accumulated in the game, you form a long-term relationship of trust and grant a sustainable motivation to play.

How it works:
1. Provide the player with a stable, dependable infrastructure of accumulated progress.
2. Make it clear that a temporary break does not lead to a loss of progress.
3. Frame the act of taking a brief rest from the perspective of "recharging and tuning your build."
4. Help the player easily settle back on track based on their existing progress whenever they return.
5. Provide reassuring return-support presentation when the user comes back or continues toward a goal.

Example analysis:
- When a player who has been maintaining a check-in streak tries to end their session for personal reasons, the system shows a flexible UX.
- Before the screen transition, the notice "Your legendary reward box, to be granted on tomorrow's login, is safely waiting. Continue whenever it's convenient for you!" is displayed.
- The box is shown reassuringly as a refined golden silhouette, and the "Schedule a reminder" and "Exit comfortably" buttons are composed symmetrically.
- The player feels not the pressure of a deadline but the reassurance that their reward is being carefully protected, and shows higher brand loyalty on return.

Diagnostic questions:
- Is the value the player has already obtained robustly maintained even when they rest comfortably at any time?
- Is the guidance popup serving its proper function as useful timeline information for the user?
- Are the path to rest and the path to continue equally and transparently visualized?

title: Building Anticipation and Challenging Reward Presentation
body:
Reward design that balances predictability and uncertainty creates the delight of exploration and roguelike-style variation, adding vitality to the game.

How it works:
1. Introduce a diverse spectrum and probabilistic fun into the reward-acquisition structure.
2. Compose the pre-reveal presentation in a refined and dramatic way to heighten immersion.
3. Honestly portray a situation where the top tier was narrowly missed, while visualizing the accumulated milestone for the challenge itself.
4. Clearly and honestly guide retry or remedial paths.
5. Tastefully package repeated challenge actions as part of a strategic growth routine.

Example analysis:
- When the player opens a rank box, the screen smoothly focuses and a refined visual effect of a rotating card is presented.
- After the top-tier card presentation crosses over elegantly, the higher-tier result honestly appears.
- The result screen, along with positive feedback such as "A great attempt!", transparently shows the upward trend of the "pity safety gauge (guarantee mechanism)" accumulated so far.
- The audio maintains a brisk, rich chord that makes one anticipate the next challenge, rather than the frustration of failure.

Diagnostic questions:
- Are the reward probability information, the pity-guarantee system, and the relief measures for duplicate rewards clearly disclosed?
- Even when the target tier is not reached, does the user appropriately accumulate real growth-milestone value?
- Does the splendor of the presentation honestly match the actual value of the item provided?

title: Balanced Hurdles and Growth Options
body:
A growth-plateau segment is a "strategic momentum" that demands new judgment from the player. Present reasonable alternatives that let the user adjust the pace of growth to suit their own play style.

How it works:
1. Provide a natural learning curve through modulation of difficulty (tension and release).
2. Design depth of fun — manual control and strategy knowledge — to be valuable even on the free, standard path.
3. Propose paid options for users who want fast progress as a transparent and attractive alternative.
4. Concentrate strong congratulatory feedback and achievement presentation immediately after a hurdle is overcome.
5. Let the user feel the fun of regulating their own pace of growth.

Example analysis:
- The user enters a particular hurdle stage and faces a boss's new pattern.
- As the enemy's attack power and gimmicks are raised, the importance of manual control is emphasized.
- At the same time, the route for equipment upgrades farmable within the game is clearly marked on the map.
- On one side of the screen, a "growth boost package" tab that can maximize time efficiency is neatly proposed.
- Whether the user finds the strategy themselves or makes use of a booster, on clear, a powerful growth-graph burst presentation maximizes the joy of a proactive breakthrough.

Diagnostic questions:
- Does the placed hurdle demand new strategic fun or an advancement of mastery from the player?
- Does the standard farming and free path give meaningful fun of experimenting with builds, rather than tedious simple repetition?
- Does the introduction of paid options function as a "time saving and expansion of choices" according to user taste, rather than the removal of a coercive barrier?

title: Belonging and Community Synergy
body:
Warm community bonding and cooperation are the most core assets of a live-service game. Design the flow so that mutual respect and dependable solidarity are brought into play in the process of achieving a common goal.

How it works:
1. Assign mutually complementary roles to guild common goals, raid balance, and the rank system.
2. Design so that an individual's participation gives the whole team a "plus-alpha" of positive synergy.
3. Send messages of warm solidarity and a welcome to participate through in-game UI, badges, notifications, etc.
4. Even in a non-login situation, use push notifications to gently convey news of events with one's precious companions.
5. Brand community participation not as an obligatory defensive battle but as a delightful festival that everyone gathers to enjoy together.

Example analysis:
- The reward for guild raid content is calculated by an accumulated-contribution bonus method.
- Even if a particular guild member cannot participate for a day due to a busy schedule, the overall reward tier is stably protected through the efforts of other guild members or a "guild safety charge currency."
- On that user's profile, instead of pressuring phrasing, a welcome badge is activated reading "Welcome anytime! Your companions have sent warm cheers."
- The push notification is displayed kindly, without provoking guilt: "A new festival has begun in the guild room. Join us when you're free!"

Diagnostic questions:
- Is a buffer in place so that an individual's unavoidable rest does not lead to a drop in the whole guild's score or to damaged relationships?
- For limited content or seasonal rewards, are re-run and alternative acquisition routes flexibly open later?
- Does the community notification give the user a pleasant sense of belonging rather than psychological burden?

title: Friendly UI and Cognitive Comfort
body:
The finest UI provides a comfortable cognitive structure in which the player feels no fatigue in any situation and can control the screen exactly as they intend.

How it works:
1. Optimize the exposure frequency of popups, notification badges, and suggestion-confirmation windows to keep the screen pleasant.
2. Design all choice options (proceed/hold/close, etc.) to be visually balanced and clear.
3. Compose the layout so that the options to decline or defer are also respected as default options.
4. Build an interface environment in which the user can sufficiently absorb information and judge at their leisure.
5. Record and honor every kind of decision the user makes as a normal and wise choice.

Example analysis:
- After failing to clear a stage, a continue/revive popup is activated.
- In the center, an "Instant revive (consumes currency)" button and a "Return to town (resupply)" button are placed at balanced sizes and a tidy transparency.
- Even if "Return to town" is pressed, instead of a warning phrase, the message "Resupply safely, then challenge again! Your precious inventory has been protected" appears.
- When revive is selected, immediate and gorgeous presentation fully supports a speedy return to combat.

Diagnostic questions:
- Are the paths to close, hold, cancel, and decline as visually and spatially clear and easy to find as the paths to proceed/accept?
- For actions that consume major assets or currency, are sufficient cancellation guidance and reconfirmation mechanisms provided in a balanced way?
- Is a cooldown system applied so that the same guidance suggestion is not exposed repeatedly, causing cognitive fatigue?

title: Salience, Sound & Motion Honesty
body:
The Salience Balance of Visual Presentation:
A refined engagement system honestly matches the intensity of visual effects to the actual importance of the content, giving the player high trust.

| Presentation Form | Sustainable Mode (the goal) | Intuition-Reinforcing Value |
|---|---|---|
| Golden spark burst | The moment of clearing a high-difficulty boss raid or achieving a monumental feat | Perfectly synchronized with the value of the actual achievement, maximizing catharsis |
| Rainbow color by tier | Intuitively conveys the actual rarity and intrinsic value of items and content | Eliminates the possibility of misreading information and secures visual clarity |
| Milestone timer | Honestly shares accurate deadline information for a live event | The user can leisurely plan their participation to fit their own schedule |
| Smart guidance badge | Exposes primarily new content and essential confirmation info the user has custom-configured | Reduces visual noise on the screen and maintains a comfortable cognitive state |
| Pulsing activation animation | Guides the core flow the player most urgently needs right now | Reduces confusion in choosing and provides natural play navigation |

The Honesty of Sound Design:
Sound is the fastest gauge by which the user emotionally interprets their own play results. Excellent audio design honestly reflects the value of the result, raising trust.
- Provide sound feedback that matches the nature of the result, helping the user clearly grasp the current situation.
- At a fork in the choice (both accept/hold), give each button its own attractive, clean feedback tone to improve the feel of control.
- The pre-reveal sound build-up of a box is precisely mixed to balance with the actual value range of the reward.
- Timer or deadline guidance sounds convey information with a calm, refined rhythm rather than creating anxiety.

The Balance of Motion and Easing Timing:
The motion of on-screen objects should be a refined signpost that guides the user as to which information is important.
- Apply smooth animation easing to accept and decline, proceed and hold buttons in a balanced way to resolve visual asymmetry.
- Place all options with the player's convenience and safety as the top criterion; the default focus is on the action the user most intuitively wants.
- Clearly provide a result-presentation skip function, guaranteeing a comfortable command for users who want a fast pace.

title: Copywriting and Positive Framing
body:
Wording is a core UX element that respects the user's choices and turns every activity in the game into a voluntary journey.

- Clear value explanation: "If you select this option, you will move to the resupply map, and your existing buffs will be safely maintained."
- Respect-based framing: "A new raid you can join with your companions has opened. Join in when you're ready!"
- Granting agency: "This is a notice about a season-limited benefit. You can check it again anytime in the Shop tab later."
- Growth-oriented wording: "Explore a new opportunity for growth!"
- Neutral options: "Check later" / "Start now"

title: Transparent Information Architecture
body:
The information architecture must be open so that every user can access the data they need easily and fairly.

- An item's price, detailed acquisition probabilities, conditions of use, withdrawal criteria, etc., are marked clearly and prominently on the screen just before the choice.
- For probability-based content, an intuitive tooltip or an official guidance page accessible with a single touch is provided by default.
- A symmetrical and transparent layout is built so the time/cost efficiency of the standard farming route and the growth-acceleration route can be compared at a glance.

title: Analysis of Representative Engagement Scenarios
body:
5-1. Check-in Milestones (Login System)

Surface experience:
- On each daily login, a charming attendance stamp is marked and a welcome reward is granted.
- Special themed rewards are provided at specific milestone day counts (7 days, 30 days, etc.).
- As the calendar fills up, your own steady record is visualized, giving satisfaction.

Engagement structure:
- Even if you take a day off, progress is not reset but safely maintained.
- It exercises flexibility so the user can keep filling the calendar whenever they return.
- The check-in system functions not as an obligation but as "a daily welcome and gift of gratitude."

Healthy design values:
- A free-pass voucher (an attendance grace day) can be granted regularly, or missed days can be gently recovered through in-game play.
- A check-in streak is merely a bonus concept; the balance is set so it never becomes an essential condition that governs core growth.

5-2. Thrilling Reward-Opening Presentation

Surface experience:
- When opening a loot box, there is the joy of anticipating what wonderful gear might appear.
- Through gorgeous visual/auditory presentation, the very process of obtaining a reward becomes a form of play.
- On obtaining a higher tier, a congratulatory message is shared across the community and the whole world.

Engagement structure:
- The acquisition probability of rewards at all tiers is transparently disclosed, so the user enjoys predictable fun.
- Even if the desired result does not appear, milestone points for the sincere attempt itself are honestly accumulated.
- The result screen does not excessively force an immediate retry and comfortably opens the flow to check your bag or to resupply.

Healthy design values:
- A guaranteed-acquisition system (pity) is clearly visualized, so the user's effort is safely protected.
- Duplicately obtained items are converted 100% into another core currency or useful value through a dedicated conversion system.

5-3. Level-Design Modulation and Growth Boosters

Surface experience:
- You encounter a dynamic plateau segment (a boss monster's appearance, etc.) and begin strategic deliberation.
- You either enjoy deep farming to your taste, or shorten the time with a booster package to break through comfortably.
- Right after a successful breakthrough, you watch a grand level-up presentation and feel deep catharsis.

Engagement structure:
- The plateau segment, having gone through meticulous balancing, is used as a stage to experiment with the fun of manual control and attribute combinations.
- The paid booster option is designed not as coercion but as a "smart time-saving alternative" for the busy modern user base.
- It excludes deliberate fatigue induction by the system, and whichever path is chosen, the user is celebrated for a "wise breakthrough" they achieved themselves.

Healthy design values:
- The rise in difficulty meshes precisely with a learning curve that stirs the player's desire for challenge.
- The intrinsic fun and reward efficiency of the in-game free alternative routes (side quests, daily dungeons, etc.) are preserved at a high level.

title: User-Friendly Signals and Optimization Alternatives
body:
| In-Game Area | Optimized Good Signal (the goal) | Value Innovation and Alternative Application |
|---|---|---|
| Attendance and check-in | A temporary absence does not damage accumulated milestones | Grace days provided, return-celebration rewards, play-based recovery routes installed |
| Reward-acquisition presentation | Refined, transparent presentation that matches actual value | Probability/pity value always specified, milestone accumulation per number of attempts |
| Content options | Presents diverse overcoming alternatives tailored to the user's situation (time vs. manual play) | Provides choices centered on diversifying convenience, appearance, and play style |
| Guidance and popups | Accept, hold, and close buttons are all designed equally and clearly | Cooldown guaranteed for a once-declined suggestion; completeness of "Don't show again" |
| Notifications and push | Refined to focus on pure information delivery and event news | Provides a user-custom notification cadence setting; pleasant welcome messages |
| Ranking and competition | Visualized around the player's individual growth metric and improvement vs. yesterday | Eases the stress of an absolute decline and encourages a personal best |
| Guild and community | The system compensates so an individual's rest does no harm to the group score | An automatic-participation buffer; an alternative-contribution (currency donation, etc.) system in place |
| Audio feedback | The actual value and emotional grain of every play result perfectly match | Even on skip, the crisp click-feedback tone inherent to the action is preserved |
| Interface motion | Tidy easing presentation considerate of information importance and the user's gaze flow | Avoids artificial pulsing effects that emphasize only certain options |

title: Engagement Simulation and Balance Criteria
body:
Before introducing a new engagement system or UI design into a live environment, establish simulation safety criteria to scientifically validate the cognitive load and satisfaction the user will feel.

Essential principles:
- Protection of real user data: During the testing stage, make no change whatsoever to the user's actual account information or owned currency.
- Prior notice and consent: When conducting a focus group test (FGT), clearly disclose the system's design purpose and obtain voluntary participation.
- Provision of debriefing: After the test ends, transparently explain to the user the cognitive-engineering principles the layout was intended to use.
- Symmetry verification via A/B testing: Compare the intuition-reinforcing UI and the existing UI side by side to objectify the figure for the comfort the user feels.
- Clarity of evaluation purpose: The purpose of the simulation is not a simple rise in metrics, but "securing the balance point between the player's proactive fun and UX stability."

title: UX Ethics and Completeness Checklist
body:
8-1. System and Content Structure

- [ ] Is the core progress safely preserved even when the player takes a break and returns?
- [ ] Does the long-term accumulation system give comfortable vitality without pressuring the player's daily life?
- [ ] Are the in-game hurdles precisely balanced to stir the user's strategic fun and mastery?
- [ ] Do the paid options function as a "pure expansion of choices" and "time-saving convenience" with coercion excluded?
- [ ] Is the presentation intensity of the reward loop honestly proportional to the actual value of the item?

8-2. The Symmetry of the UI/UX Choice Structure

- [ ] Are all paths — proceed, hold, check later, close — equally clear visually?
- [ ] When declining or holding a particular option, does it avoid repeatedly raising unnecessary reconfirmation windows?
- [ ] Are an item's cost, acquisition probability, and conditions of use transparently understood before the user makes a choice?
- [ ] Do the "always-on cooldown" and "don't show for a week" functions, considerate of the user, work flawlessly in the guidance-popup system?
- [ ] Are the color, size, and font visibility of all buttons balanced so they do not hinder the user's proactive judgment?

8-3. The Honesty of Juice Feedback

- [ ] Does the achievement-celebration presentation correctly match the scale of the accomplishment the player actually achieved?
- [ ] When a disappointing result occurs, is it expressed honestly without glorifying it with an exaggerated upward presentation?
- [ ] Even when a resupply option is chosen, does the system give feedback that wholeheartedly supports the user's wise judgment?
- [ ] Does the in-game sound synchronize excellently with the meaning of the actual event happening on the current screen?
- [ ] Do all guidance motions and timings help, above all, the user's visual comfort and cognitive ease?

8-4. A Community Environment of Mutual Respect

- [ ] Does the guild and companion system avoid violating or punishing the individual's full right to rest?
- [ ] Is a remedial measure in place so that non-participation in particular content does not lead to public burden or alienation within the guild room?
- [ ] For season-limited rewards, has a flexible re-run or alternative farming route been left open during the live-service period?
- [ ] Does the competition and ranking UI operate in a way that encourages the individual's growth pace rather than inducing impatience in the user?
- [ ] Is every live push notification delivered not as a mere login lure, but as useful information and a joyful invitation to the user?

8-5. Final Quality-Determination Questions

> Does this design help the player actively enjoy the game because they "genuinely want to" in every moment?

> Does the Juice on this screen sensorially amplify the joy of the pure achievement the player has earned?

title: Button feedback: 5-state spec
body:
The button is the most basic yet most frequently used interaction element in games. Every game's Juice starts with the button.

State machine:
Normal -> Hover -> Press -> Release -> Normal
                                     -> Disabled

Spec by state:
