Normal (default state):
- Default color, default size (100%)
- Subtle breathing animation while idle (optional): Scale 100% <-> 101%, 2-second cycle

Hover (mouse over / focus):
- Target size: 110%
- Easing: EaseOut, Duration: 0.15 sec
- Color: 5% brighter
- Optional: add a subtle shadow

Press (while pressing):
- Start size: 110% (Hover), target size: 95%
- Easing: EaseOut, Duration: 0.05 sec (immediate)
- Color: 10% darker

Release (moment of release):
- Start size: 95%, target size: 110% -> 100%
- Easing: Back (slight overshoot), Duration: 0.2 sec
- Sound: play click sound
- Haptic: short single vibration (10ms)

Disabled (inactive):
- Color: 50% saturation reduction (Grayscale or Desaturate)
- Opacity: 60-70%
- Block interaction, no cursor change

Mobile-specific considerations:
- No Hover state -> immediate visual change needed on Press
- Touch area: minimum 44x44pt (Apple HIG) / 48x48dp (Material Design)
- Touch feedback: provide visual + haptic simultaneously

title: Popup/Modal entrance, dim, exit
body:
A popup must appear suddenly to grab the player's attention, but if too abrupt, it startles or displeases.

Entrance animation (Scale + Fade + Back easing):
- Start state: Scale 0%, Opacity 0%
- Target state: Scale 100%, Opacity 100%
- Easing: Back (Overshoot 0.3)
- Duration: 0.3 sec
- Result: the popup feels like it "pops" out

Background dim treatment:
- Semi-transparent black background (50% opacity)
- Background Fade In: 0.2 sec
- Purpose: focus attention + block interaction with the UI behind

Exit animation (Scale Down + Fade Out):
- Start: Scale 100%, Opacity 100%
- Target: Scale 80%, Opacity 0%
- Easing: EaseIn
- Duration: 0.2 sec (faster than entrance)

Principle: Entrance slow and exaggerated, exit fast and quiet.
Reason: the entrance must grab the player's attention, so exaggeration is needed, but the exit must not disrupt the flow.

title: Number change: count-up, damage numbers, crits
body:
When a number such as health, score, or gold changes, an instant change makes it hard for the player to perceive "how much it changed."

Number counting animation:
- Example: 1000 -> 1500
- Animation: 1000 -> 1100 -> ... -> 1500
- Increment interval: 0.05 sec, total Duration: 0.25 sec
- Easing: EaseOut (fast at first, slow at the end)
- Optional: Scale 120% -> 100% (the number grows then returns)
- Pitch build-up on combos/streaks: each time the count steps up, raise the tick SFX pitch a little progressively to create a sense of rising tension. (Peggle's score tally and Mario's consecutive coin pickup sound are the canonical examples.) This is a different technique from the +/- random pitch used to avoid monotony (Game_Feel_Juice_Bible_EN T8): randomization prevents repetition fatigue, build-up creates a sense of rise.

Damage number popup:
- Entrance: Scale 0% -> 120% -> 100%, Duration: 0.15 sec
- Color: normal=white, critical=yellow/red
- Movement: rise 50px upward, Duration: 0.5 sec
- Exit: Fade Out (the last 0.2 sec)
- On multiple hits: prevent overlap with positional offsets

Additional critical hit presentation:
- Scale: 150% -> 100% (start larger)
- Screen Shake: 10px, 0.1 sec
- Sound: additional impact sound
- Color: yellow outline + glow effect
- Haptic: strong single vibration (50ms)

title: Health/progress bars: dual-response, segments, warnings
body:
Dual-response bar system (on getting hit) — one of the most refined techniques in game UI design:
- Immediate-response bar (red): moves instantly (or EaseOut <=0.1 sec) to the target value when damage is taken
- Delayed-response bar (white / 'chip' bar): catches up to the target value over ~0.4 sec with EaseOut
This dual structure intuitively shows the player "how much they were hit." The gap between the red bar and the white bar is the amount of damage taken.

Sequence (Health 50 -> 30):
1. 0.00 sec: The red bar instantly (or EaseOut <=0.1 sec) moves to 30. The white bar stays at 50.
2. 0.00 sec: The health bar border flashes red (0.1 sec)
3. The white bar catches up to 30 over ~0.4 sec with EaseOut (arrives ~0.40 sec)

On increase (Heal / XP): if a decrease shows "how much you were hit" via the dual-response bar, an increase should show the satisfaction of filling up.
- Instead of snapping instantly, fill up over 0.2-0.3 sec with EaseOut.
- Flash the bar bright once at the moment the change starts, to emphasize "it just went up."
- Heal = greenish; XP = the gauge's own color + extra flourish when a level-up is imminent (tie into the charge/full light burst).

Segmented (discrete) bar — dividing health/stamina into cells can make the count felt rather than just seen:
- Each time a single cell's value changes, give that unit a small pop (Scale punch) + click sound.
- When several cells change at once, stagger them 0.03-0.05 sec so they tick sequentially -> the magnitude is conveyed tactilely.
- Keep filled and empty cells in clear contrast to aid readability of the remaining amount.

Low-health warning:
- Health 25% or below: red pulse ('red breathing') + desaturation + warning sound + subtle vibration (1-2px tremor)
- (Optional) the more critical it gets, the faster the pulse cycle, to heighten urgency

Charge / skill cooldown bar:
- A bright color fills up over a dark background
- Just before full: pre-announce with a particle effect
- On full: light burst + "ready" sound + icon bounce
- Icon Scale: 100% -> 120% -> 100%, Duration: 0.2 sec

title: Notification/Toast timing and priority
body:
Entrance:
- Slides from off-screen into the screen
- Easing: EaseOut, Duration: 0.3 sec

Hold:
- 2-5 sec (depending on content length)
- Important notifications persist until the player closes them

Exit:
- Slide off-screen or Fade Out
- Easing: EaseIn, Duration: 0.2 sec

Priority management:
- High importance: display immediately, push other notifications aside
- Low importance: add to a queue, display sequentially
- Repeats of the same type: combine and display as "x3," etc.

title: Inventory/items: acquisition, rarity tiers, equip
body:
Item acquisition presentation:
1. The item is sucked toward the player (0.3 sec, EaseIn)
   - Trail particle afterimage
2. The inventory icon glows (0.5 sec)
   - Icon Scale: 100% -> 120% -> 100%
   - Light particles around the icon
3. Play acquisition sound
4. Display acquisition notification

Differentiated presentation by rarity:

| Grade | Visual Effect | Screen Shake | Sound | Haptic |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Common | Default presentation | None | Default acquisition sound | Light (10ms) |
| Uncommon | Green glow | None | Default + rising melody | Light (20ms) |
| Rare | Blue glow + particles | Weak (3px, 0.1 sec) | Special acquisition sound | Medium (30ms) |
| Epic | Purple light burst | Medium (5px, 0.15 sec) | Dramatic melody | Medium (50ms) |
| Legendary | Golden burst + light streaks | Strong (10px, 0.2 sec) | Fanfare | Strong (100ms) |

On equip:
- The equip slot glows
- Equipment-application animation on the character preview
- Equip-complete sound

On unequip:
- Animation of the item moving from the slot to the inventory
- Unequip sound

title: Screen transitions by type
body:
Principle: every screen transition must have an animation. An abrupt transition unnecessarily stimulates the Salience Network, causing displeasure. (For the Salience Network, see the neuroscience section of UIUX_Visual_Theory_Trends_EN.)

Spec by transition type:

| Transition Type | Technique | Duration | Easing |
|---|---|---|---|
| Moving between screens at the same level | Slide (left/right) | 0.3 sec | EaseInOut |
| Parent->child screen | Slide (bottom->up) or Scale In | 0.3 sec | EaseOut |
| Child->parent screen | Slide (top->down) or Scale Out | 0.25 sec | EaseIn |
| Scene transition | Fade to Black (or custom) | 0.5-1.0 sec | EaseInOut |
| Entering combat | Dramatic transition (wipe, shatter, etc.) | 0.5-1.5 sec | Custom |

title: Microinteractions: purpose-driven, invisible
body:
Trend: in 2026, microinteractions evolved to be more refined and purpose-driven. Moving beyond mere decoration, they perform the roles of conveying information, prompting action, and emotional reward.

Game application:

| Microinteraction | Information Conveyed | Emotional Reward | Example |
|---|---|---|---|
| Pull-to-refresh spinner | Signals loading | Reduces the boredom of waiting | Custom loading character animation |
| Like/heart burst | Confirms the action | Joy, achievement | Particle burst + sound |
| Toggle switch flip | Confirms state change | Physical satisfaction | Squash & Stretch + click sound |
| Progress bar completion | Confirms task completion | Sense of accomplishment | Light burst + celebration sound |

Core principle: a microinteraction should be felt but not consciously noticed. If the player consciously thinks "this animation is nice," it's already overdone. Natural is good.

title: Multimodal feedback: integrated design matrix
body:
Trend: in 2026, integrated design of visual, auditory, and tactile feedback became the standard. Rather than designing each channel individually, you define the responses of all three channels simultaneously for a single event.

Integrated design matrix (example: hit event):

| Intensity | Visual | Auditory | Tactile | Temporal |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Weak (normal hit) | Hit flash 0.05 sec, 5 particles | Impact sound (mid) | Weak vibration 10ms | — |
| Medium (heavy hit) | Hit flash + knockback, 20 particles | Impact sound (bass-reinforced) | Medium vibration 30ms | Hit pause 0.05 sec |
| Strong (critical) | Hit flash + knockback + Screen Shake 10px, 40 particles | Critical SFX + bass boost | Strong vibration 50ms | Hit pause 0.1 sec |
| Extreme (finisher) | Full-screen effect + zoom in + Screen Shake 20px | Dedicated finisher SFX + slow | Vibration pattern 100ms | Hit pause 0.15 sec + slow motion |

For detailed parameters of each channel's individual techniques (Screen Shake, hit pause, haptics, etc.), see Game_Feel_Juice_Bible_EN Part II.

title: Ethical feedback: choice symmetry and cognitive comfort
body:
Feedback must be a tool that respects the player, not one that persuades them. Lay out every choice path intuitively and symmetrically, disclose information transparently, and honor every player decision as a wise choice.

The symmetry of the choice structure — every choice the player faces (proceed/hold/decline/close) must be equally clear both visually and spatially. The UI does not force any particular decision and supports every alternative with equal weight.
- Decline and defer are also default options: do not hide or weaken options such as declining or "check later"; place them with the same visibility, size, and tidy transparency as the accept button.
- Symmetric easing: apply smooth animation easing to the accept and decline, proceed and hold buttons in a balanced way to resolve visual asymmetry. The default focus is on the action the user most intuitively wants.
- Each button its own feedback: at a fork in the choice (both accept and hold), give each button its own clean feedback tone to improve the feel of control.
- Balance of reconfirmation: for actions that consume major assets or currency, provide sufficient cancellation guidance and reconfirmation, but do not repeatedly raise unnecessary reconfirmation windows on a declined or held choice.

Example: a continue/revive popup after failing a stage — in the center, place "Instant revive (consumes currency)" and "Return to town (resupply)" at balanced sizes and transparency. Even for "Return to town," instead of a warning phrase, show "Resupply safely, then challenge again! Your precious inventory has been protected."

Cognitive comfort of popups & notifications — the finest UI is a comfortable cognitive structure in which the player can control the screen exactly as they intend, without fatigue in any situation.
- Optimize the exposure frequency of popups, notification badges, and suggestion-confirmation windows to keep the screen pleasant.
- Cooldown system: a once-declined suggestion is not re-shown immediately (an always-on cooldown). The "Don't show again" / "Don't show for a week" functions work completely.
- Notifications and push are refined to focus on useful information and event news rather than a mere login lure, and a user-custom notification cadence setting is provided.
- Build an environment in which the user can absorb sufficient information and judge at their leisure, and record and honor every user decision as a normal and wise choice.

Related principles — positive copywriting/framing, transparent information architecture, and UX research ethics — pair with the topics copywriting_framing, information_transparency, and engagement_validation.

Ethics & Symmetry Checklist:
- [ ] Are all paths — proceed, hold, check later, close — equally clear visually?
- [ ] When declining or holding, does it avoid repeatedly raising unnecessary reconfirmation windows?
- [ ] Are an item's cost, acquisition probability, and conditions of use transparently understood before the user makes a choice?
- [ ] Do the "always-on cooldown" and "don't show for a week" functions work completely in the guidance-popup system?
- [ ] Are the color, size, and font visibility of all buttons balanced so they do not hinder the user's proactive judgment?
- [ ] Do guidance motions and timings, above all, help the user's visual comfort and cognitive ease?

title: Neuroscience-Based UI Design (ECN-DMN Framework)
body:
Game UI is not merely displaying information; it interacts directly with the player's brain. The human brain has three core networks, and the way these networks are activated determines the player experience.

ECN (Executive Control Network) — the region of the brain activated when performing goal-directed tasks. It operates "when you must focus and get something done."
Situations where the ECN is activated in games: aiming at and firing on an enemy in an FPS; pressing a button at the exact timing in a rhythm game; reading a boss's attack pattern and dodging it; landing a skill combo during a MOBA team fight.
Player state when the ECN is active: high concentration, fast reaction speed, short decision-making time, high energy expenditure (fatigue if sustained).
UI Design Principles:
| Principle | Description | Concrete Criterion |
|---|---|---|
| Immediate feedback | Minimize the delay between input and reaction | Within 100ms |
| Clear visual hierarchy | Place important information where the eye naturally goes | Health/skill cooldowns at the bottom of the screen or near the character |
| Minimize cognitive load | Limit the number of UI elements | 7±2 simultaneously displayed pieces of information (Miller's Law) |

DMN (Default Mode Network) — the region of the brain activated when the brain is not focused on an external task. It relates to imagination, daydreaming, self-reflection, and future planning.
Situations where the DMN is activated in games: aimlessly admiring scenery while exploring an open world; freely conceiving of structures to build in Minecraft; immersing in a story and imagining a character's emotions.
Player state when the DMN is active: relaxed and unhurried, enhanced imagination and creativity, low energy expenditure (can be sustained for long periods).
UI Design Principles:
| Principle | Description | Concrete Criterion |
|---|---|---|
| Minimize UI | Game content occupies most of the screen | Game content 80%+ |
| Provide contextual information | Show information only when needed | Health bar shown only during combat |
| Smooth transitions | Apply animation to UI entry/exit | Fade In/Out 0.2–0.3 sec |

Salience Network — plays the role of switching between the ECN and DMN. It judges "is this information important right now?" and decides whether to switch to focus mode (ECN) or maintain relaxed mode (DMN).
UI Design Principles:
| Principle | Description | Concrete Example |
|---|---|---|
| Pre-announce transitions | Provide a signal to the player before a mode change | Tense music precedes an enemy's appearance |
| Buffer interval | Provide a 2–3 second cushion on an ECN→DMN transition | After a combat victory: victory presentation → reward → resume exploration |
| Forbid abrupt transitions | Prevent mode switches without warning | No abrupt QTE insertion during a cutscene |

UI Strategy by ECN-DMN Balance:
| Game Character | ECN:DMN Ratio | UI Strategy | Feedback Characteristics |
|---|---|---|---|
| Hardcore action | 80:20 | High information density, combat UI always displayed | Intense and immediate, aggressive use of Screen Shake |
| Balanced RPG | 50:50 | UI shown in combat, minimized while exploring | Intensity adjusted to the situation |
| Healing/casual | 30:70 | Minimal UI, information provided contextually | Soft and relaxed, strong effects withheld |
| Mobile MMORPG | 60:40 | Core info always visible + supplementary info collapsible | Clear feedback per interaction, touch-optimized |

title: Cognitive Load Theory
body:
Human working memory has a limit on the amount of information it can process at once. Applying this theory to UI design lets you keep players from wasting unnecessary mental energy.

Three Types of Cognitive Load:
| Load Type | Definition | Game Example | UI Design Strategy |
|---|---|---|---|
| Intrinsic Load | Load arising from the inherent complexity of the task itself | Reading and dodging a boss pattern | Managed through difficulty tuning. Cannot be solved with UI |
| Extraneous Load | Load arising from elements irrelevant to the task | Complex UI, meaningless animation | Must be removed via UI/UX optimization |
| Germane Load | Beneficial load that aids learning and understanding | Feedback telling you "what you did well" | Reinforced by providing appropriate feedback |

UI Footprint Guidelines:
- UI should occupy only 30% or less of the screen.
- If UI occupies 40% or more, Extraneous Load increases and fatigue rises.
- Exception: Strategy/simulation games may have a high UI ratio due to large information volume, but the priority of information must be made clear.
- Mobile exception: The UI ratio may rise to secure touch areas, but visual footprint must be managed with collapsible/tab-based structures.

title: 2026 UI Trends: AI Adaptive, Motion, Diegetic, Intent-Based
body:
TR1. AI-Based Adaptive UI
The biggest change in the 2026 UI/UX field is the emergence of Generative UI, where AI generates and adjusts the UI in real time. Rather than a fixed interface, it provides a UI that dynamically changes according to the user's behavior, context, and proficiency.
Game application:
- Adaptive difficulty UI: show more guidance UI to beginners, and automatically minimize it for experts
- Context-based information density: auto-activate only combat-related UI during combat, only map-related UI during exploration
- Personalized feedback intensity: auto-adjust Juice intensity based on the player's play time and reaction patterns (slightly lowering effect intensity the longer they play, to manage fatigue)
Design principle: AI is a copilot, not autopilot. When AI changes the UI, it must always have lower priority than the player's manual settings. AI does not overwrite values the player has explicitly set.

TR2. The Maturation of Motion Design
In 2026, motion design's role shifted from "decoration" to "communication." The purpose of animation is not "to look good" but to convey state, structure, and system intent.
Key changes:
1. Functional motion: transition animations are a means of conveying information, not mere decoration
2. Mandatory reduce-motion option: respect the prefers-reduced-motion setting. Users with vestibular disorders, attention differences, or other sensitivities must be able to turn off strong motion
3. Artificial Delay: intentionally adding a brief delay in form submissions, etc., makes users more strongly confident that "the change has been reflected"
Game application:
- Always provide an intensity-adjustment slider for strong motion such as Screen Shake and hit pause
- Add an intentional 0.2–0.5 sec delay + a progress animation to important actions like saving/equipping
- Every UI transition must have a purpose: if you can't answer "why is this animation here?", remove it

TR5. Diegetic UI
A design where UI elements exist naturally within the game world. Instead of overlaying the HUD on top of the screen, it integrates them as objects in the game world.
Representative cases: Dead Space — the health bar is a spine light attached to the character's back; Sky: Children of the Light — UI blends naturally with the environment.
Game application:
- Eliminate the transition between menus and gameplay to maximize immersion
- For 2D top-down: express health as an aura/color around the character, and the inventory as floating icons near the character
- Especially effective for DMN-activating games (healing, exploration)

TR7. Intent-Based Design
The 2026 UX paradigm shift: you don't design an interface, you design the outcome. The focus is not on "which screens the user passes through" but on "what the user is trying to accomplish."
Game application:
- Quest UI: not a quest-list screen, but automatically displaying "what to do next" from the player's current situation
- Shop UI: not listing all products, but prioritizing recommended items matched to the player's current equipment/level
- Settings UI: not listing all options, but proposing solutions based on the current problem (lag, dizziness, etc.)

title: Glassmorphism & Liquid Glass, Neumorphism 2.0
body:
TR3. Glassmorphism & Liquid Glass
A style that mimics frosted glass with semi-transparent panels, blurred backgrounds, and subtle borders. As Apple's "Liquid Glass" design language spread across all platforms including iOS and macOS, it became a mainstream trend in 2026.
Game application:
- Applying a semi-transparent effect to HUD panels softens the boundary between the game world and UI, maintaining immersion
- Caution: on low-spec devices, the blur effect is a performance burden. On mobile, limit the blur radius or use a static blur texture
- For text legibility, always maintain sufficient contrast (contrast ratio 4.5:1 or higher) on semi-transparent panels
Parameter Guide:
| Property | Recommended Value | Notes |
|---|---|---|
| Background opacity | 60–80% | Too transparent degrades legibility |
| Blur radius | 10–20px | Mobile: 10px, PC: 20px |
| Border | 1px, white 20–30% opacity | Glass-edge feel |
| Shadow | 0 4px 30px rgba(0,0,0,0.1) | Sense of floating |

TR4. Neumorphism 2.0
An evolved version that maintains a soft, tactile feel while solving the first generation's accessibility problems (low contrast, unclear boundaries).
Game application:
- Apply soft UI only limitedly to core touchpoints like buttons and sliders
- Use it as an accent, not a full app skin
- Suitable for places needing softness, such as the settings screens of health/wellness and casual games
- Mix with flat elements to provide contrast: neumorphic button + flat form field

title: Accessibility-Centered Design (Baseline Requirement)
body:
TR6. Accessibility-Centered Design
In 2026, accessibility was elevated from a "nice-to-have" to a baseline requirement.

Game Application Checklist:
| Item | Implementation Method |
|---|---|
| Color-blindness support | Don't convey information by color alone. Pair with icons/patterns |
| Font size adjustment | UI text size adjustable in settings |
| Control remapping | All control keys reconfigurable by the user |
| Reduced-motion mode | Off option for Screen Shake and camera shake |
| Subtitles/captions | Subtitles for all voice. Size/background color adjustable |
| Touch area | Minimum 44×44pt (iOS) / 48×48dp (Android) |
| Contrast ratio | Text contrast minimum 4.5:1 (WCAG AA) |
